skill.name: hybrid-search-implementation
skill.description: Build hybrid search systems that combine vector similarity, keyword search, rank fusion, and reranking for RAG applications.
skill.summary: Guides implementation of hybrid search for RAG using vector search, BM25, RRF fusion, and reranking.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, search terminology, and resource links. The audit found a real SQL injection risk in the PostgreSQL template, where metadata keys are interpolated into SQL, so publication should wait for a safer example.

high_findings.title: SQL Injection Risk in PostgreSQL Filter Template
high_findings.description: The PostgreSQL example appends filter metadata keys into a SQL WHERE clause and then interpolates that clause into conn.fetch. Values are parameterized, but keys are not validated or quoted, so untrusted filter names could alter the generated SQL.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant code directly inserts metadata key names into SQL text before executing the query. This is a classic identifier injection issue, even though filter values use parameters.

medium_findings.title: Potential Elasticsearch Script Injection in Example Query
medium_findings.description: The Elasticsearch example inserts boost_vector into a script_score source string instead of passing it as a script parameter. The type hint says float, but copied code may receive untrusted runtime input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in copyable code, but the parameter is annotated as a float and may be controlled only by trusted application code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code fences and Python examples, not Ruby backtick shell execution. No executable shell command behavior is present in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line-numbered evidence shows Markdown fences and section transitions. I did not find Ruby backtick execution at these locations.

low_findings.title: Credential and Weak Crypto Findings Are Vocabulary Matches
low_findings.description: The sensitive, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance findings match words such as keyword, embedding, method, code, and index in search documentation. No certificates, private keys, hash routines, or system probing commands are shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain ordinary hybrid search terms and result ranking code. I did not find credential access, cryptographic operations, or host reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Resource Links and Database Fetch Are Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The network findings include a database fetch call inside an example and public documentation links. They do not send credentials or local data to an unauthorized endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch call is asyncpg database access and the URLs are resource citations. I did not find evidence of credential exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated Identifier Interpolation in SQL
dangerous_patterns.description: The template builds where_clause from metadata keys and injects it into a SQL query. Safer code should allowlist metadata fields or use database-safe identifier quoting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The vulnerable interpolation is visible in the example, and the generated clause is executed by conn.fetch.

dangerous_patterns.title: Inline Script Construction for Elasticsearch Scoring
dangerous_patterns.description: The example builds an Elasticsearch script source with an interpolated boost value. Passing boost values through script params would reduce injection and parsing risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The source string interpolation is explicit, but the argument is intended to be numeric application configuration.

content.value_statement: Single search methods often miss exact terms or semantic matches. This skill provides implementation patterns that combine vector search, keyword search, fusion, and reranking.
actual_capabilities: Explains when hybrid search improves retrieval quality.
Provides reciprocal rank fusion and linear score fusion patterns.
Shows PostgreSQL examples using pgvector and full-text search.
Shows Elasticsearch examples using dense vectors and BM25 matching.
Outlines a custom RAG pipeline with reranking support.
limitations: Examples require adaptation before production use.
The PostgreSQL metadata filter example needs safer identifier handling.
It does not benchmark retrieval quality on your dataset.
It does not include deployment, monitoring, or access control guidance.

use_cases.title: Improve RAG Recall
use_cases.description: Add keyword matching beside vector retrieval so product names, codes, and exact phrases are not missed.

use_cases.title: Design Search Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare RRF, weighted scoring, reranking, and cascade patterns before building a search service.

use_cases.title: Prototype Database Search
use_cases.description: Start a hybrid search prototype using PostgreSQL, pgvector, or Elasticsearch as the retrieval backend.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Fusion Method
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a first hybrid search implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to compare RRF and linear fusion for my RAG search use case. Recommend a starting approach and explain tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt a PostgreSQL Prototype
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer building with pgvector and full-text search.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a PostgreSQL hybrid search design with pgvector, full-text indexes, safe filters, and ranking output.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Search Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Team improving search metrics after a prototype.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to propose a tuning plan for vector weight, candidate count, RRF settings, and reranking evaluation.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user preparing a hybrid search service for launch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my hybrid search architecture for performance, security, observability, failure modes, and evaluation gaps.

output_examples.input: A team wants better retrieval for legal documents with exact citations and semantic questions.
output_examples.output: A recommended architecture using BM25 for citations, vector search for semantic recall, RRF fusion, and reranking for final ordering.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to balance vector and keyword results in a RAG pipeline.
output: Start with RRF because it needs less score calibration.
Log separate vector, keyword, and fused ranks.
Evaluate on representative queries before changing weights.

output_examples.input: A backend engineer needs a production checklist for hybrid search.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering indexes, query filters, safe parameter handling, candidate limits, reranker latency, monitoring, and offline evaluation.

best_practices: Use representative queries and labeled results to tune fusion settings.
Log vector rank, keyword rank, fused score, and rerank score separately.
Validate filters and use allowlists for database field names.
anti_patterns: Do not copy example SQL filters without adding safe identifier handling.
Do not assume vector search alone handles exact identifiers well.
Do not add reranking without measuring latency and quality impact.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build search systems that combine semantic vector retrieval with keyword matching for stronger RAG recall.

faq.question: Which backends does it discuss?
faq.answer: It includes examples for PostgreSQL with pgvector, Elasticsearch, and a custom RAG pipeline.

faq.question: Is the code production ready?
faq.answer: No. The examples are starting points and need security, testing, monitoring, and performance work.

faq.question: Why use hybrid search instead of vector search only?
faq.answer: Hybrid search preserves exact term matching while still finding semantically related content.

faq.question: Does it include reranking?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how a cross-encoder reranker can reorder candidates after fusion.

faq.question: What is the main audit concern?
faq.answer: The PostgreSQL metadata filter example needs safer handling for dynamic field names before use.
